tool_notes: CPU-side stack sampling. `mlx.metal.start_capture()` was also tried for kernel-level breakdown but the .gputrace bundle was too large to fully parse (4.2 GB AX, 3.2 GB mlx_lm). The MetalLib was inspected for kernel inventory; sample(1) gives the actionable CPU-side comparison.

workload.seed_baseline: seed varies per AX call (1234..1273); mlx_lm uses default
workload.ngram_policy: AX: max_output=32 < NGRAM_MIN_OUTPUT_FOR_ACCELERATION(64) → n-gram skipped (direct same-policy)

absolute_speed_caveat: AX measured 131.8 tok/s vs mlx_lm 108 tok/s in this specific harness, opposite to ADR 0024's framing. This is NOT a clean A/B because (a) different seeds per iteration on AX (b) mlx_lm uses generate() which re-encodes the prompt each call. The README artifacts cited in ADR 0024 use a different methodology. **Do not cite this absolute number** as direct-decode performance evidence — the structural overhead finding below is the relevant signal.

structural_overhead_finding.summary: AX's forward pass creates roughly 2× more mlx::core intermediate objects than mlx_lm for the same Gemma 4 E2B forward pass, and emits 1.67× more `gpu::eval` barriers. The extra work is CPU-side graph bookkeeping + sync barriers, not GPU compute.
interpretation: Dispatch count is only 10% higher (65 vs 59) — the per-token Metal pipeline calls are roughly the same.
But array/SmallVector/Dtype counts are 2–4.7× higher — AX creates many more short-lived intermediate tensors per forward pass.
gpu::eval count is 1.67× higher — AX flushes more often, meaning more small batches of GPU work instead of larger fused ones.
AX has 3 wait_for_one sync barriers in the window; mlx_lm has 0. Each barrier is a CPU stall waiting for GPU.
Pattern: AX is more imperative/eager in the forward path; mlx_lm batches more work between eval points.

ax_only_kernels_in_metallib: (split-K decomposition variants compiled into AX's MLX path but not mlx_lm's)
kernel_inventory_diff.note: Both Metal libraries contain affine_qmv_*, gemv_bfloat16_*, etc. The split-K variant presence in AX is interesting but may not be hit in the actual dispatch — that requires deeper command-buffer parsing not done here.

track_k_target.primary: Reduce per-forward-pass mlx::core::array allocation count + gpu::eval count in the Gemma 4 path. The 1.67× eval ratio + 2.26× array ratio is the lever.

candidate_interventions.intervention: Audit Gemma forward path in `crates/ax-engine-mlx/src/model.rs` for unnecessary intermediate allocations. Each MlxArray construction is one ref-counted handle + one MLX graph node; fewer is faster on small models like E2B.
Reduce eval-barrier count: identify where AX calls mx.eval() (or implicit eval via .item() / .shape() inspection) more often than mlx_lm. Each extra eval is a GPU sync stall.
Check Gemma4 per-layer-input gating path (the `per_layer_embed` / `per_layer_proj_w` pieces unique to Gemma4 2B/4B). If those add extra intermediates, that's the source.

decision_gate_status: G1 evidence collected. Per ADR 0024 D2 and PRD G1, no eval-boundary / KV-layout / kernel change should commit before this artifact justifies it. With this artifact in place, an eval-barrier-reduction or array-count-reduction track is now evidence-backed.
